data.title: Bank Margins, Earnings Set To Expand, Possibly Defy Credit Concerns
data.description:
Net interest margin expansion has driven returns higher across the banking industry in 2025. Earnings growth should continue in the coming quarters, even as credit costs continue to migrate higher.

The take

US bank earnings have continued to grow as strong margin expansion and benign credit quality supported performance. While credit costs should continue to normalize, we expect favorable results to continue, buoyed by additional margin expansion and steady loan growth. The positive fundamental landscape and a friendlier regulatory environment should spur more bank M&A activity as acquirers see deals as an attractive and faster way to gain scale — in the overall size of the company or specific markets — that they see as necessary to effectively compete.

Investors warming up as credit conditions hold for now

Many generalist investors heading into third-quarter 2025 earnings expected and looked for credit problems in banks' results. Save for a few one-offs that emerged in the period — often related to loans to nondepository financial institutions, which have driven much of the loan growth through the first nine months of 2025 — credit quality trends largely remained benign. Valuations, particularly for large banks, improved heading into 2026 as the consumer and the economy remained resilient and credit concerns eased.

We expect credit quality to normalize from current levels due to weakness in the US consumer and some stress in commercial real estate portfolios, leading to a higher level of losses. Consumer delinquencies have risen from historical lows and student loan delinquencies have jumped back near pre-pandemic levels now that the moratorium on payments is several quarters in the rearview mirror. The deterioration offers another indicator that the US consumer is stretched, marked by slower savings rates and recent weakness in the labor market.

However, bankers say that while consumer sentiment has waned, the consumer and small businesses remain resilient. Many consumers took advantage of historically low rates to refinance their mortgages before the Fed began raising rates, reducing the impact that rate hikes would have on their debt service. Consumers and other borrowers are also expected to receive more relief in the form of additional rate cuts.

Banks have seen delinquencies in their commercial and industrial and commercial real estate loan portfolios rise off historical lows, but delinquencies stabilized in the first, second and third quarters of 2025.Banks have reduced their exposure to CRE and have arguably benefited from private credit firms growing their market share and helping support the asset class.

Some skeptics argue that lenders have downplayed credit quality deterioration by modifying loans and extending maturities further out on the horizon, invoking the phrase "extend and pretend." There is evidence that extensions have occurred in the CRE market, with the number of CRE mortgages estimated to mature declining in 2025 when compared to estimates a year earlier. What remains to be seen is whether those extensions offered borrowers enough breathing room to weather the storm and if recent rate declines position them to service their debts.

We expect higher loss content in the future, but to date, few problems have occurred. In our baseline scenario, we expect provisions to dip to 19.1% of net revenue in 2025 from 19.9% through the first nine months of 2025. That ratio is expected to increase to 22.1% in 2026 as banks prepare for tougher sledding ahead.

The higher level of provisioning will serve as a modest headwind, but earnings are still expected to rise 14.6% year over year in 2025 and grow another 3.1% in 2026.

Many bankers and members of the sell-side community expect credit trends to remain stable. If that occurred, bank earnings would grow 16.2% in 2025 and another 9.8% in 2026.

Fed cuts spell funding cost relief, further margin expansion

Banks will continue to benefit from the remixing of their balance sheets as funding costs decline, while lower-yielding assets purchased or originated when rates were low, mature and are reinvested at higher yields.

Deposit costs dropped substantially late in 2024 and early in 2025 as the highest-cost deposits, often certificates of deposits (CDs) originated when rates were lower, matured and repriced at lower rates. Banks, particularly regional and community banks, increased their reliance on CDs for funding during the Fed's tightening cycle. CD pricing appears to have peaked in the second quarter of 2024 before the Fed's first rate cut in September. Since many CDs carry one-year terms, many institutions have already experienced relief from their highest-cost CDs that likely matured in the first half of 2025.

The industry failed to record the same level of declines in the second and third quarters of 2025 but has reduced its reliance on CDs for funding. Rates cut by the Federal Reserve late in 2025 were expected to offer greater funding cost relief. Fourth-quarter results from JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) and Bank of America Corp. (BAC) offered support for that thesis, with the companies' cost of interest-bearing deposits dropping 17 basis points and 21 basis points, respectively, from the prior quarter.

Banks will need to remain competitive with alternatives in the Treasury and money markets as they seek to cut costs further. The difference between the average fed funds rate and the industry's cost of deposits declined modestly in the third quarter of 2025. That gap should narrow as additional rate cuts by the Fed allow banks to lower their deposit costs while staying competitive with higher-yielding alternatives to maintain deposit growth.

However, stronger loan growth means that banks funding needs are greater, tempering just how quickly they can lower their deposit costs. We expect banks' deposit costs to decline notably in the fourth quarter of 2025 and the first quarter of 2026, but additional funding relief will be harder to come by without further rate cuts by the Fed. The central bank is expected to cut rates again later in 2026, resulting in sharper declines in deposit costs.

Lower rates will also lead to pressure on loan yields, but this pressure will be mitigated by the replacement of historical, lower-rate assets with newer, higher-yielding alternatives. That dynamic will allow for continued margin expansion.

Looking ahead

The desire to grow earnings, gain scale and a friendlier regulatory environment should support stronger bank M&A activity. Stronger banks likely will use the positive fundamental environment to invest in technology and their franchises and remain relevant, while weaker institutions could find it more difficult to compete and could look to partner with their larger counterparts.

Bank M&A activity closed 2025 with a bang after several years of depressed activity. Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) opened the fourth quarter with plans to acquire Comerica Inc. (CMA), marking just the fourth bank deal over $10 billion in value since the financial crisis. Other sizable transactions followed, including Huntington Bancshares Inc.'s (HBAN) plans to purchase Cadence Bank (CADE) for $7.59 billion and Nicolet Bankshares Inc.'s (NIC) $866 million pending purchase of MidWestOne Financial Group Inc. (MOFG). The fourth quarter ended with eight bank deals with values over $500 million announced in the period — the most in any quarter since the first quarter of 2021.

Large buyers like Huntington and active acquirers like Nicolet have returned as regulators have signaled that they are far more supportive of M&A activity. With more buyers, the desire to gain scale to invest in technology, succession issues, pent-up demand for deals during the pandemic, and the rate hike cycle, as well as stable fundamental trends, all should lead to greater bank M&A activity.

The expected pickup in deal activity among regional and community banks would alter the competitive landscape. Buyers that gain scale through deals likely would be less focused on organic growth while integrating their acquisitions. They will also be more likely to address any concerns they have in their loan portfolios since bank M&A accounting requires the buyer to mark a target's balance sheet to market. The fair value mark would, in theory, allow an institution to purge unwarranted assets without taking large additional losses.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alaska Air Is A Value Stock Worth Buying
data.description:
As you can see in the chart below, the past 52 weeks haven't been particularly kind to Alaska Air Group, Inc. (ALK). The stock has seen declines of 28% even after the December rally, and so it seems that the business is far from its best. Alaska Air reported their 2025 Q4 earnings just yesterday (Jan 22nd) and today I believe it is an opportune time to initiate coverage on the stock by looking at their results, strategy, and valuation.

In the below discussion it is shown that Q4 was generally a respectable quarter for Alaska Air. While liquidity and debt load are worth monitoring, changes for unit revenue and cost were positive. In addition to a good long-term strategy, the financial outlook for FY2026 is solid, and so I view Alaska Air stock as attractive given that the valuation is currently quite low. Therefore, I'm initiating a buy rating.

Unit Revenue Growth Outperforms

First things first, Alaska Air reported total revenues of $3.63 billion for 2025 Q4 and that missed expectations by $10.3 million. Despite the underperformance versus consensus analyst expectations, this does represent YoY growth of 3%. Note that in Q3, there was 23% of growth, but nonetheless, Q4 seemed to be a resilient quarter for the top line.

For a better measure of how efficiently Alaska Air is generating their revenues, let's take a look at the change in their RASM. As you can see in the chart above, they saw a 0.6% increase YoY. This represents a slowdown from the previous quarter, and they also missed their own expectations of ~1% growth for the metric. While this may be a bit unfavorable, note that their RASM percentage change has outperformed the Big 4 by a notable margin in the past four quarters. Even though there are signs that the Big 4 is now closing the growth gap, the ability for Alaska Air to grow RASM in 3 of the past 4 quarters is a demonstration that their revenue-generating efficiency is heading in the right direction overall. Note that if it wasn't for the U.S. Government shutdown in the quarter, results here could have been even more robust.

Unit Cost Control Has Improved

In terms of CASM percentage change when excluding fuel effects, there were generally positive developments. While the 1.3% increase in CASMex outpaced the RASM growth rate to show some shaky profitability, there has been notable improvement. Firstly, Alaska Air projected a ~3% increase for Q4 and so results show that there was better cost control than expected. Secondly, note the large drop in CASMex growth from the previous quarter. It should also be stated that 1.3% is the smallest percentage increase for unit cost in the past four quarters. That is particularly impressive considering that they were able to still grow capacity by 2.2%. Therefore, while cost control can definitely still be better, they also seem to be heading in the right direction here.

EPS Drop Less Concerning Than It Seems

For Q4, Alaska Air reported an adjusted EPS of $0.43. That is a 56% decline YoY, but this could be less concerning than it actually is. If you go and take a look at their income statement, you can see that there was a non-operating expense of $45 million versus $6 million of non-operating income in the prior year period. Therefore, the actual operating profitability of the company seems to be better than implied by the bottom line figure. Furthermore, the company was still able to beat analyst expectations by $0.32. That happened while revenues missed, so there shouldn't be too many complaints with regards to profitability.

Capital Developments

In terms of the company's financial position, there are some deteriorations that investors should be aware of. At the end of 2025, total current assets totaled $3.266 billion, and that represents a notable decline from the $3.76 billion balance at the end of 2024. Meanwhile, over the same period, current liabilities have increased from $6.145 billion to $6.589 billion, and so there has been a meaningful change in the company's liquidity. Essentially, Alaska Air's current ratio has dropped from 0.61 to 0.50. While it is normal for airlines to have lower current ratios, this deterioration is a bit eyebrow raising and investors should monitor these developments moving forward.

In the above charts, firstly, you can see that adjusted net debt to EBITDAR is a bit higher than desired. They have a target of below 1.5, and as you can see Q4 is not really a move in the right direction in that regard. The company seems a bit leveraged and that could add a bit to the investment risk. Another interesting development is the continued share repurchases. As you can see, the diluted share count has shrunk, with the company having bought back ~$30 million in stock in Q4. Why is this interesting? Well, while the company did have $185 million of operating cash flows in the quarter, they also spent $625 million on property and equipment, and so FCFs were negative. Therefore, the company is cutting into their cash pile in order to fund these buybacks. Perhaps, management believes their stock is significantly undervalued at this stage.

Strategy Looks Good

Before discussing their guidance, I want to talk a bit about their strategy. Back in late October 2025, the company announced that they will be adding originating airports on the west coast and will also add new routes to Hawaii. I believe this is a clear statement that the company is focused on expansion, and for investors, that is likely a promising direction.

As another sign that they are focused on expansion, it was reported earlier this month that Alaska Air will buy 105 Boeing (BA) 737-10s and 5 Boeing 787s. As Rob Williams of Seeking Alpha writes, this marks "the largest aircraft order in the carrier’s history and extending its delivery pipeline through 2035." In my view, this is another demonstration that Alaska Air is focused on growth.

In addition to pushing growth, it also seems that they want to improve the experience for customers. On Jan 5th, it was announced that Hawaiian Airlines would invest over $600 million over five years. As Clark Schultz of Seeking Alpha writes

The carrier plans to modernize airport spaces, upgrade technology, and retrofit aircraft interiors while also expanding community and sustainability work across Hawaii.

In my view, these are very worthwhile investments. At the end of the day, the customer experience should be one of the key areas of focus for all companies, and so I view these proposed improvements as a potential driver of long term success for the company.

Financial Outlook

Alaska Air provided guidance for both full year 2026 and for Q1 specifically. I'll focus on the annual outlook for the purposes of this discussion, as it should provide a better long term view. For capacity growth, they are expecting it to be between 2% and 3%. For the full year 2025, they saw capacity growth of 1.9%, and so there are signs that the company's expansion will accelerate a bit this year.

For adjusted EPS, they provided a rather broad range, but nonetheless, at the midpoint, they are implying growth of 105% YoY. That sure beats the 50% decline seen in 2025 from 2024 levels, and so overall profitability is expected to jump back up.

As for CAPEX, they are expecting around $1.45 billion. In 2025, they spent $1.588 billion on property and equipment, so spending also isn't a concern moving forward. As a whole, I view full year 2026 guidance as solid, and it indicates the worst is over for the company.

Value Stock

At current levels, I would say that Alaska Air is likely well within value stock territory. In the above chart, you can see that the forward P/E ratio is now back below 10 and given the solid financial outlook and growth strategy that the company has, I would say that there is considerable undervaluation right now. If you go and take a look at Seeking Alpha's valuation page for the stock, you can see that Alaska Air is currently trading substantially below its own 5 year average based on many different valuation multiples. Given the seemingly improving fortunes of the company, I would say that the risk/reward is currently in a good position for the bulls, as stock outperformance is a very real possibility moving forward.

Recap

Alaska Air clearly hasn't had an easy past year, but their Q4 results show that the company has been resilient and is performing in a decent manner, all things considered. As discussed earlier, liquidity and debt load could be improved moving forward but since the strategy and near term financial outlook are robust, I view their prospects favorably. With that in mind, I would say the value stock valuation provides a significant amount of opportunity, and so I'm initiating Alaska Air stock at a buy rating.

data.title: Record Amount Of Utility Rate Requests In 2025 Amid Affordability Concerns
data.description:
US investor-owned energy utilities requested rate increases from state regulators totaling $22.06 billion in 2025, as tracked by Regulatory Research Associates. This is the highest amount of nominal requests since RRA started tracking rate case proceedings in the early 1980s. Rate requests pulled back slightly in 2024 to $16.39 billion after three years of rising requests in 2021, 2022 and 2023.

The take

  • Another year of record utility rate requests was established in 2025. After filing record-setting rate requests in 2021, 2022 and 2023, utilities moderated their proposals in 2024, with requests totaling $16.39 billion. But in 2025, that total jumped to $22.06 billion, the highest annual total of electric and gas rate increase requests since RRA began tracking rate cases.
  • Rate requests in 2025 were again dominated by electric utilities, which requested $18.23 billion in aggregate rate increases, while gas utilities requested $3.83 billion. The electric rate filings reflect the utilities' significant capital expenditure plans for upgrading transmission and distribution (T&D) systems and installing new renewable generation and technologies to accommodate the clean energy transition, as well as fossil and nuclear generation, to support large loads. The gas filings continue to reflect investment to replace aging infrastructure. Inflation and the rising cost of capital continue to be highlighted as factors in recent case filings.
  • The growing prevalence of multiyear rate plans is a large driver of the aggregate increase. Some of the largest rate requests in the period were attributable to multiyear plans from Florida Power & Light Co. (FPL) and Consolidated Edison Co. of New York Inc. (CE)(ED), which seek to invest in sustainability, safety and reliability, support growth and storm-hardening efforts.
  • Historically, rate case filings have been more numerous in the late spring and early summer months, while more rate case decisions are usually rendered later in the year.

These general rate cases and limited-issue rider proceedings have been driven by the need to invest in infrastructure to upgrade aging T&D systems; build new gas, solar and wind generation; and implement new technologies, including smart meters, smart grid systems, cybersecurity measures, infrastructure to support electric vehicles and battery storage. The rate requests reflect the move to shutter aging fossil fuel plants amid a nationwide push toward clean energy and protect customers against growing climate-related risks such as wildfires and damaging storms. Most recently, growing needs for investment in T&D and generation to support large load customers, including data centers, have been behind some requests.

Rate requests in 2025 amounted to the highest annual total ever tracked by RRA. The busiest time for new rate case filings tends to be late spring or early summer, with the least activity observed in the fall. By contrast, the fourth quarter is generally the busiest time of the year for rate case decisions, particularly in December.

The 2025 total was 35% above the total requests in 2024 despite a 13% drop in total case filings in 2025. Rate increases requested by utilities in 2025 were 413%higher than the amount sought in 2020 at the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic, when many utilities withdrew or postponed rate cases to reduce the economic impact on struggling businesses and residents.

Rising inflation, particularly for labor and costs related to the supply chain, has been a constant topic of discussion in ongoing rate cases. For example, FPL, which filed the largest rate ask in 2025, cited higher operations and maintenance expenses tied to inflation and customer growth among the key factors driving revenue requirements in 2026. FPL indicated that costs incurred in support of the capital investments have been impacted by historic inflation and the unexpected significant migration to Florida from around the country, underscoring the importance of maintaining financial strength and flexibility 2026–29.

There are regulatory constructs in certain states that may protect investor-owned utilities from rising costs. For more information, refer to State regulatory constructs offer insight on inflation threat to utility income.

Continuing wildfire mitigation efforts and storm hardening investments are driving rate case activity, particularly in the West. Public Service Co. of Colorado (PSCO) filed an application Nov. 21, 2025, seeking a $526 million rate increase. PSCO states the increase request is primarily driven by $2.068 billion of electric distribution plant additions and associated costs since its last rate case; technology and nonlabor wildfire mitigation operations and maintenance expense; and recovery of seven deferrals totaling $283.3 million. The deferrals are related to advanced grid initiatives, excess liability insurance, revenue decoupling, the 2020 wildfire mitigation plan, EV charging infrastructure, innovative clean technology projects and coal combustion residuals.

In addition to FPL and CE, some of the largest rate requests were made by Pacific Gas and Electric Co. (PCG), Duke Energy Carolinas LLC (DUK), Virginia Electric and Power Co., Duke Energy Progress LLC and Oncor Electric Delivery Co. LLC.

Number of cases

There were 135 new electric and gas rate cases, including limited-issue rider proceedings, filed in 2025, down from 156 initiated in both 2024 and 2023.

With high inflation and higher interest rates, rate case decisions remained elevated in 2025 with about 151 cases decided, down slightly from 155 cases decided in 2024, according to RRA data.

Available initial data highlights a slight slowdown in rate case activity in 2026. As of Dec. 16, 2025, RRA-covered companies have announced an additional 25 planned rate case filings through 2026. By comparison, companies had announced the filing of 30 new rate cases as of the same date in 2024. For more information, refer to Inflation, capital spending drive strong US rate case activity.

Affordability concerns persist

As we enter 2026, affordability remains a key issue shaping the US power and utility sector, moving from a background concern to a binding constraint on what utilities can earn and build. This shift is driven by mounting pressure at state and federal levels, with energy prices having risen much faster than inflation and customer dissatisfaction at historic highs. Utilities are navigating multibillion-dollar capex programs, which risk further upward pressure on bills unless offset by new strategies. The upcoming election cycle and federal policy discussions are amplifying the focus on affordability, with industry groups and utility executives highlighting the need to moderate rate growth and maintain customer trust. RRA is watching closely how affordability concerns could reshape regulatory and market dynamics.

In response, regulatory styles are evolving, with commissions increasingly adopting frameworks that force utilities to balance investment and operational efficiency within strict limits. The sector's reliance on data centers as growth drivers is becoming more pronounced, with these customers now bearing a larger share of costs, a trend that utilities must communicate transparently to regulators and the public. States with politically exposed commissions are tightening scrutiny on utility spending and bill impacts, while federal agencies like the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission and Department of Energy are expected to play a more active role in transmission planning and reliability funding.

Cognizant of rising affordability concerns, utilities have been taking steps to highlight for commissions their efforts to reduce costs and improve efficiency even as they ask for higher rates. In a rate case application filed Nov. 25, 2025, South Jersey Gas Co. (SJG) indicated it conducted an affordability analysis, which it says concludes that its prices for gas services are currently affordable for most residential customers and will remain affordable if the proposed rate change is approved. SJG included $4.3 million in annual savings and other cost reductions in its revenue request and uses competitive bidding and cost tracking to ensure capex are reasonable.

The surge in requested rate increases comes amid rising capex by the energy utility sector. 2025 is anticipated to be another record year for utility capital investments, with the aggregate forecast capex of $214.293 billion for the 47 tracked energy utilities when results are tallied, according to an RRA analysis. This figure would exceed the $173 billion spent in 2024 by about 23%.

Spending is driven by pent-up demand to replace and modernize aging infrastructure, as well as renewable portfolio standards of multiple states that call for large expansions in low-carbon energy generation capacity. Additionally, federal infrastructure investment plans aim to convert the nation's power generation network to zero-carbon sources while investing in new generation to support strong electric growth. For the most recent report on capex, refer to Energy utility capex forecast nears $1.2 trillion in 2025–29.

Data centers — which underpin AI, digital services and cloud infrastructure — are rapidly becoming a significant new driver of power demand in various regions. This trend is expected to result in substantial capital investments for years to come, including massive investment in natural gas generation, new nuclear capacity and reviving retired coal plants or those planned for retirement. In the US, demand from data centers is projected to increase at double-digit rates through the end of the decade, fundamentally altering the overall demand landscape and placing additional strain on utility electric grids.

Multiyear rate change proposals

Legislation passed at the state level over the past several years has driven an increase in multiyear rate change requests, whereby utilities can request incremental rate increases over several years. RRA considers these plans to be a constructive ratemaking approach. In 2025, at least 16 rate cases that were filed requested multiyear rate changes. That is down from 25 such cases filed in 2024 and 21 multiyear rate case filings in 2023, which was equal to the number of multiyear requests filed in both 2021 and 2022.

Just as in 2024 and 2023, some of the largest rate cases filed in 2025 were multiyear requests. In aggregate, FPL initiated a large rate case (Docket 20250011-EI) with the Florida Public Service Commission, seeking a multiyear increase in electric base rates totaling $2.472 billion between 2026 and 2027. The rate increase is intended to fund investments to support system growth and reliability, diversify the company's power generation fleet, add solar generation and energy storage capacity, and improve resilience and security at critical facilities. On Nov. 20, 2025, the PSC voted to approve a settlement agreement authorizing FPL a $1.65 billion rate increase in 2026 and 2027, or two-thirds of FPL's ask.

Long-term perspective

From a historical perspective, the nominal $22.04 billion requested by utilities in 2025 significantly exceeded rate requests in the early 1980s, when the country was undergoing a major boom in baseload plant construction. Prior to 2021, the highest requested annual rate increase total occurred in 1981, when utilities put forth petitions for about $13.1 billion in rate increases. Adjusting for inflation, however, the rate requests in the early 1980s still outpace current rate requests.

Comments on RRA's methodology

RRA endeavors to follow all "major" rate cases for investor-owned utilities nationwide, with major defined as a case in which the utility's request would result in a rate change of at least $5 million or in which the commission approves a rate change of at least $3 million. In addition to base rate cases, the rate case history database includes details regarding certain limited-issue rider proceedings, primarily those that involve significant rate base additions that are recognized outside of a general rate case. In some of these cases, the rate change coverage criteria may not apply.

Moreover, some US utilities operate under formula-based rate plans where rates are adjusted annually (or on some other periodic basis) without a full rate case filing and generally without revisiting the authorized return. These proceedings are generally not included in the database and, therefore, would not be included in the tally of cases or the aggregate rate change stats. Details are discussed in the footnotes to the case in which the formula rate plan was established.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cal-Maine Foods: Zero Debt, Massive Cash Position, And Ready For GLP-1 Tailwinds
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Cal-Maine Foods (CALM), I highlighted their ability to turn the recent volatility into long-term value while maintaining a zero-debt position and expanding into prepared foods that can enhance their predictability and average margin throughout the cycle, turning a cyclical commodity company into a more diversified business.

Following the recent report, this ability is proven once again, with high triple-digit growth in their prepared foods operations, as they are developing rapidly into higher value-added products (prepared foods) while maintaining a spotless balance sheet with zero debt and over $1.1 billion in cash, while the ~14% fall since the previous article on top makes this even more compelling, justifying a reiterated Buy rating.

Internal Developments

As a result of the environment normalizing following the hike in egg prices due to the bird flu, CALM's net sales dropped 19.4% to $769.5 million in Q2'FY26, with shell egg sales down 28.1% to $649.6 million, but their prepared foods up a whopping 586.4% to $71.7 million, as the company is actively developing in the value-added segments.

The company’s margins and volumes are now normalizing following the significant jump we saw due to last year’s avian flu, while their diversification efforts got their specialty eggs and prepared foods to 46.4% of net sales in Q2’FY26, compared to 31.2%, with the management highlighting the following during their earnings call:

With lower egg prices, our increasingly diversified business model, combined with effective execution has proven to be a source of resilience and that positions us uniquely today, a rare combination of both value and growth with the potential to strengthen even further over time.

This highlights something very important about CALM, as the company has been able to build a very strong cash position while also developing their business to build a more diversified portfolio, with significant growth (30%) expected in prepared foods over the next 18 to 24 months - all while still maintaining zero debt so far.

In other words, we could say that CALM is now ready more than ever to face the downcycle, as fellow Seeking Alpha analyst Ragmar Rikberg also mentioned in his recent coverage.

Of course, all of that is achieved while still being by far the #1 egg producer and distributor in the US, with a leading market share and a strong, vertically-integrated distribution network and customer relationships.

Financially, based on CALM’s latest report, we continue to see an excellent position, with the current assets covering their total liabilities several times, while holding over $1.1 billion in cash and short-term investments. Combined with being debt-free, this places CALM in an exceptional position, being ready to take advantage of a potential downturn to expand while others are facing weakness, providing significant upside potential relative to the ~$3.89 billion market cap.

As for the shareholder returns, CALM continued to adapt it to the current environment, with the dividend dropping to $0.719 per share based on the latest announcement, for an annualized yield of about 3.5%. Meanwhile, the buybacks reached $75 million as of Q2’FY26 (part of their $500 million authorization), while the company spent very significant amounts on M&A (acquisitions seen in the second screenshot above) and CAPEX YTD, as they are still investing significant amounts.

Macro Developments

Right now, the overall consumer environment is still under pressure, with several recent studies pointing out the high levels of stress seen going into 2026 due to factors such as lower income and rising costs of living.

Again, it’s important to understand that this is a natural response to the rate hikes seen a few years ago, as the reason behind this tool being used is to slow down the economy to fight inflation, which consequently lowers the consumer’s discretionary budgets.

As highlighted in many recent analyses of companies from various industries (from auto to food and beverages), consumers are reportedly trading down, with de-premiumization trends being visible as people go for the better value propositions as a result of the general pressure they feel, while some economists hint at a K-shaped economic growth, with the higher-income households “masking” the pressure felt by the lower-income ones, although that can be visible if we look at the performance of dollar stores like Dollar General (DG) or Dollar Tree (DLTR) - covered recently here and here.

Meanwhile, the BLS reported that egg prices continued to fall in December, with a 20.9% drop YoY and 8.2% compared to November, which should continue to pressure CALM’s numbers even further, with the stock closely following these prices so far.

However, CALM remains in a strong position to take advantage of this, as eggs remain an essential source of protein - especially now after prices fell - and could even see some tailwinds from the Make America Healthy Again policies, while the recent Dietary Guidelines change further emphasizes their importance for the current administration.

Recently, we’ve seen more studies supporting the idea that GLP-1 could turn out to be a threat for the food industry, as it’s estimated to cause grocery spending to fall by ~5.5% six months after taking it (at least 1 individual from the household), while higher-income households reported a higher, 8.6% drop compared to 4.2% for lower-income. Again, given the effects of the medicine, this shouldn’t be a surprise, but having quantifiable data can help the market model its impact much more efficiently.

Although I don’t see it as a massive threat for the general food industry by itself - I see more risk for beverage/snack producers like PepsiCo (PEP) due to the “war” against ultra-processed food we might see unfolding - this could actually be a positive for CALM. CALM’s CEO also highlighted the following during their Q2’FY26 Earnings Call, pointing out that their consumers are actually trading up, which is very rare in this market:

What makes this platform particularly compelling is how the category and consumer behavior are evolving. Across the U.S., eggs remain an affordable protein source. Consumers are seeking complete high-quality proteins. GLP-1 users are gravitating towards satisfying nutrient-dense foods, younger consumers and families are treating eggs as an everyday staple. And across the board, convenience is a major tailwind with rising interest in ready-to-eat and ready-to-heat formats. We see consumers trading up.

Although there’s not enough data behind this just yet, I believe it’s reasonable to expect that eggs and egg-based prepared foods could actually benefit from a more “health-aware” population, as they remain an excellent source of protein and nutrients that’s also affordable (under normal macro conditions), easy to cook and very versatile.

If anything, if the current pressure on the consumer continues, we could even see more demand for CALM’s products, as consumers could switch away from eating out and move more towards cooking at home due to the current prices and affordability issues.

Meanwhile, CALM should also offer some protection against all the fear seen in the market, with rising tensions following the Greenland situation, renewed tariff fears, Fed independence worries, etc., all of which can have an effect on the broader economy, while the company has zero debt and isn’t directly affected by a potentially higher-for-longer rate environment.

Not only that, but to add another layer of defense, the company’s core operations are actually in private labels, which is normally seen as a risk for legacy brands as consumers are trading down to the store-owned brands, while CALM is nowhere near as affected, providing another layer of defense.

Risks Reiterated

Still, even though they can prove to be defensive in a weak environment like this going forward, they still depend significantly on the volatile price of eggs, which play an essential role for the company’s margins and operations as they are technically a commodity stock.

Even though they can benefit from long-term health trends, lower spending, a declining population, and more consumer issues can still affect them and especially the market’s expectations, which can translate into weaker valuations, especially if further macro pressure persists.

Valuation

For CALM’s valuation (using the DCF to Enterprise Value method in this case to capture the significant cash position), I’ll go for a more conservative base free cash flow of $150 million, although I believe the new financial position, inflation, and significant recent investments warrant an upgrade compared to the pre-pandemic averages, basically assuming a FCF conversion rate of about 4.87% based on the current revenue (annualized) throughout the cycle.

I’ll assume that this is followed by a CAGR of 4.5% over the long-term thanks to long-term macro improvements in the consumer environment (health trends and recovery), inflation and significant potential thanks to their expansion and massive cash position that can be reinvested, followed by a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years as they continue expanding into higher-margin operations, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a WACC of 8.5% to take into account their volatility as a commodity company, with $1.14 billion in net cash and 48.17 million shares outstanding as of their Q2’FY26 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $2.87 billion, for an equity value of $4.01 billion and an intrinsic value of $83.15 per share, slightly above the current $81.57, although we should take into account that the company can continue improving well above these expectations given the significant long-term tailwinds and financial flexibility.

Conclusions

I reiterate CALM’s Buy rating, with the company’s rapid ramp-up in higher value-added products and excellent financial position offering strong potential to reshape the business in the long term.

Still, even though they will depend on egg prices, I believe the overall risk-reward is in favor of the investment, especially if we take into account their financial flexibility and the potential macro tailwinds.

data.title: Bombardier: Strong Growth, But Valuation Risk Is Rising (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Bombardier (BDRAF)(BDRDF), the Canadian business jet specialist, have surged 36.7%, exceeding my price target. The stock price has now surged almost 1,200% since I upgraded the stock to buy in June 2022. The performance has been simply impressive. However, we note that with the significant appreciation in the stock price and the stock having surpassed my price target, valuation risk is also on the rise. In this report, I discuss the expectations for Bombardier's Q4 2025 earnings, the longer-term growth drivers for 2026 and beyond, and I reassess the stock price target and rating.

Bombardier Q4 Performance Expected To Reflect Backloaded Delivery Profile

For the fourth quarter, analysts are expecting revenue to hit $4.82 billion, marking 8.4% year-over-year growth. The estimates range between $4.68 billion and $4.92 billion and increased 1.6% over the past six months and decreased 0.5% over the past three months, with revisions on revenues being split evenly between upward and downward revisions. The fourth quarter traditionally is the strongest quarter, with the most deliveries and consequently also the highest revenue performance and cash flow.

Earnings per share are expected to be $4.68 marking 8.7% growth, with estimates ranging from $4.08 to $5.56. Over the past six months, estimates increased 18%, and over the past three months, estimates improved 7.3% with eight upward revisions to the estimates and two EPS revisions downward. I believe this is driven by the company showing a strong margin trajectory as well as managing its debt prudently, driving financing costs lower.

The Growth Drivers for Bombardier

For Bombardier, the expectation currently is that 2026 will not see a major boost in airplane deliveries. Whether the company has changed its mind on the production output in 2026 will be one of the watch items during the discussion of the Q4 results and outlook, as we also note that the company is selling slots in the 2028-2029 frame. That means that at some point, if the company cannot offer customers near-term deliveries, order inflow and associated cash inflows will slow down.

However, the lack of committed upside in airplane production does not necessarily mean that we will not see growth in 2026 and beyond. The first Global 8000 was delivered in December 2025 with a rising number of deliveries expected in 2026 for a combined higher output of Global 7500/8000 jets, which should result in program margins improving. Additionally, demand in the defense market is increasing, which could bring some high margin programs to work on. One example of defense order inflow came in December when Bombardier announced that Canada had placed an order for six multi-role aircraft for the Canadian Air Force with a value of $400 million and expected completion in 2027. We do note that due to low quantities, these high margin programs could cause some volatility.

This year, we should also see the opening and expansion of the service centers in Indiana and Abu Dhabi with a full annualized effect from 2027 onward. The company has also been proactively repaying and refinancing debt, resulting in $409 million in annualized savings in interest costs.

So, we see revenue and profit upside due to airplane production mix, revenues from a higher services footprint, and defense opportunities. This has allowed the company to repay debt from free cash flows and reduce interest costs, further improving the quality of the business.

Easy Upside Has Materialized For Bombardier

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Bombardier, the challenging part in valuing the stock is that there are no pure-play business jet stocks, and the company’s median multiple carries some noise from the turnaround. We also note that if we look at multiples of companies that do manufacture business jets, the companies with the most depressed median multiples have the highest EPS growth prospects. So, there is some valuation risk for Bombardier, but we note that the complete aerospace and defense peer group trades at around 24x EV/EBITDA.

Based on the expectations for the 2025-2027 timeframe, we no longer see upside at the median EV/EBITDA multiple of 10.8x. As a result, I am downgrading the stock from strong buy to buy. My price target is based on expected 2025 earnings valued in between the peer group and Bombardier’s median, implying a $228 price target with 17% upside. So, we note that the investment case is now predominantly hinging on the expansion of the trading multiple. Given Bombardier’s execution, I do believe there is a defensible case for that.

For Bombardier, the expectations are that margins will rise from 15.3% in 2023 to 17.5% in 2027. Cash flow performance in 2025 is inflecting strongly as the business is scaling up, margins are improving, and the most demanding CapEx years are in the rearview mirror. The company is expected to end 2025 in the healthy 2x-2.5x range for aerospace and defense companies. That should also help the company to refinance debt at lower interest rates, and we also note that this potentially opens up the opportunity for shareholder returns, but I would prefer further debt reductions over shareholder returns. By 2027, the company’s cash position is expected to grow to $2.9 billion. Assuming that the company can operate the business with $1.5 billion in cash, that would provide another $1.4 billion that can be directed towards debt repayments to drive interest cost savings.

Conclusion: Bombardier’s Valuation Risk Is Real, So Is The Earnings Growth

Bombardier has definitely strengthened the business and is continuing that process with higher cash flows being used to bring down the debt balance. That has been a multi-year process, and I do not believe the company will stop there. At some point, dividends and shareholder repurchases should be possible, driving up the appeal of the stock. However, we also note that against the median EV/EBITDA of 10.8x, there is no tangible upside, and that means that the upside I expect is largely driven by an increase in the trading multiple as Bombardier continues to trade at a steep discount to its peers. That effectively means that some margin of safety is gone, and I am adjusting the rating accordingly from “Strong buy” to “Buy.”

data.title: Koninklijke Philips: Macro Set-Up Favors And AI Tailwinds
data.description:
With Koninklijke Philips N.V. (PHG), we started the year with a neutral stance after the JPM Healthcare Conference and upgraded the stock to buy in July 2025. With the analysis called Turning The Page With Recovery Signals, as litigation-related uncertainties fade, and FCF turns upward, our conviction strengthened, leading us to upgrade the rating. In numbers, Philips shares increased by 21.19% (Fig. 1).

Fig 1

Why Are We Positive?

At the macro level, we need to report that Healthcare is the third-largest sector in Europe. This represents 13.4% of the STOXX Europe 600 and comprises 52 constituents, behind only Financials (23%) and Industrials (19%). It has been one of the laggards over the past three years (with only Autos and Food & Beverage performing worse), which has pushed valuations to notably attractive levels. Here at the Lab, we believe 2026 will likely mark the start of a revival for EU Medtech and a return of generalist interest. On a relative basis, comparing the sector’s P/E multiple to that of the broader market, healthcare has been cheaper than the broader market only in 2009–2010 during the global financial crisis and in 2020–2021 around the COVID-19 outbreak. The underperformance of the STOXX 600 since 2022 makes the valuation genuinely attractive.

We need to report that, since 2021, the company has trailed market growth, largely due to the Sleep recall. However, we see a positive trajectory. Philips' execution should strengthen as the portfolio becomes more competitive and supply-chain disruptions fade. In our numbers, we see a 5% organic revenue CAGR through 2030. Looking at Q3, order intake growth is up 8% (and top-line sales are up 3%) (Fig. 2), so we expect a sales acceleration ahead. After the Q3 results in early December, the company released evidence of " continued performance improvement with sequential comparable sales growth, expanded margins (despite tariff headwinds) and strong cash flow." This is related to the 2026 expectation. At the same time, the top end of management’s mid-to high-teens margin ambition looks ambitious. We believe the company will benefit thanks to operating leverage, incremental cost savings, and product/segment mix. Therefore, we see a profitable growth with margin expansion. We expect Q4 results to come in broadly in line with consensus. For 2026 guidance, we believe the company can achieve a 12.0–12.5% margin range, leaving scope for Wall Street upgrades over the year. Over the medium term, we see a core operating profit margin at 15%, which would translate into EPS upside versus consensus, but this goes beyond our 12-month target price horizon. So, we are positive on both the short- and long-term.

Source: Philips Q3 results ppt - Fig 2

The latest Philips deal on AI cannot go unnoticed. The company agreed to buy SpectraWAVE. This is a player that "develops advanced imaging tools for coronary arteries and uses AI to support medical imaging and heart assessments." Here at the Lab, thanks to our deep-dive work, we believe AI is going to be a key beneficiary of Medtech. These tailwinds are both clinical and economic. AI integration into imaging (Fig. 3), diagnostics, and remote monitoring is already improving accuracy. Key to the report is the fact that Philips already holds leading market positions with data, regulatory know-how, and clinical validation. Over time, this will likely translate into a higher growth rate, better margins, and, consequently, higher capital efficiency.

Fig 3

Earnings Changes And Valuation

Rolling forward our estimates for 2026, and backed by our analysis, we believe Philips can achieve 5% sales growth and a 12.5% margin (Fig. 4). There is no change from our previous note, so we continue to see CAPEX on sales of 6% and D&A of €1.3–1.4 billion. So, adding numbers, Philips sales, EBITDA, and EBIT are estimated at €18.6 billion, €3.6 billion, and €2.3 billion, respectively. In our view, the company is not fully valued. Assuming no M&A, the company has the potential to deleverage at least €500 million per year. We are also reducing the company's interest expense from €250 million to €240 million. Considering a corporate tax rate of 23% (aligned with the company's historical average), our net income projection is €1.58 billion. Our EPS was €1.64 based on the current outstanding shares.

With no change to our valuation methodology, we applied a FWD 20x P/E target. This represents a 15% discount relative to the Sector Median and aligns with the company's 5-year historical average (Fig. 5). Therefore, we increased our target price from €27.2 to €32.8 ($38.5 in ADR). We also believe the business is undervalued on an EV/EBITDA basis. In our estimates, Philips trades with an EV/EBITDA of 8.1x, while the MedTech segment trades on a sector average of 12x.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Risks

Risks applicable to Philips include development risks due to delays, product efficacy, and increased competition from new products and technologies. Philips is also exposed to commercial risk and regulatory risk. Aside from new approval, there are patent risks to consider that might lead to significant market share/price erosion. Philips might be involved in other potential litigation. As already reported, the company is exposed to FX change. Here at the Lab, we also have to consider China's demand and more moderate growth in emerging markets.

Conclusion

All in all, we believe Philips has grown and has margin upside potential. At the macro level, the company is supported by a return of interest in the Medtech industry and tailwinds from AI. With a compelling valuation, our buy is then confirmed.
