data.title: Arista Networks: AI Is Not Enough Anymore, Execution Matters
data.description:
Thesis

On January 22nd, Arista Networks (ANET), jumped up almost 15% on opening, before closing out the day at approximately 8%. While the jump was large, it wasn't a coincidence, as it occurred right after BNP Paribas' publication of their Top AI picks for 2026, which listed ANET, along with Celestica and Credo Technology Holdings, as an "AI pick" due to their data center visibility, now extending far beyond just the short term. This is simply confirmation that the AI infrastructure cycle, although appearing to slow, has actually never stopped; it has merely transitioned into a new phase. I have previously discussed Arista and its performance in the past, and I have found its recent performance to be fascinating.

I have long believed that Arista is an important stock within the overall infrastructure space; it is not a story of hype, but rather a function of its role within the value chain. It produces network products where there are real bottlenecks today. Regardless of how fast or slow AI may be advancing at times, there will continue to be data center investment spending occurring, as we have seen for some time now. As such, the market has regained confidence in the company. If one wishes to buy a stock that rose by 15% and does not understand why it did so, they are wrong. First, we need to determine if the data center visibility continues in 2026. If so, the company still has a chance to continue to advance.

Earnings

I have previously written about how Arista sells (the customer base), what Arista sells (the products), and where Arista sells (geographically), so revisiting that again would provide little value. Therefore, I will use this opportunity to discuss something potentially more relevant to people currently looking at the stock: what happened in Q3 and what I anticipate occurring in Q4.

Arista Networks' Q3 2025 earnings were announced in November, approximately three months ago; therefore, I am using Q3 as a reference point not to evaluate whether the company is executing well, but to understand what caused the market reaction and, most importantly, if there are established performance objectives that need to be met. Q3 was positive, and as always, I encourage readers to review the detailed breakdown in the Seeking Alpha section. This format works best for me when conducting my own analysis since it delivers a quick summary of everything.

I do not see any indication in the Q3 numbers that the business is slowing down operationally or experiencing cracks in its model. Specifically, revenue increased by 27.5% YOY, reaching $2.308B with non-GAAP EPS of $0.75. Also, the company continues to maintain high margins, including the GAAP gross margin of 64.6% and the non-GAAP margin of 65.2%.

However, the stock fell after the quarterly results. Investors have been concerned about something. I'm talking about the Q4 guidance.

I didn’t hear any alarm bells about revenues, I think the continued strength of $2.3-$2.4 billion in revenues indicates strong demand, not a softening. The issue has never been revenue. The issue has always been margins. The company provided guidance for a non-GAAP gross margin of 62-63% and an operating margin of 47-48%, lower than what was seen in Q3. At this point the market started asking questions.

After the company provided its guidance, the market asked if the AI acceleration is resulting in a less favorable margin mix, or if the current trend is simply due to timing issues (e.g., ramping up new products) and/or short-term pricing actions. To me, the second explanation is the most reasonable explanation: that while the AI narrative hasn’t dissipated, it has certainly cooled down, and it has become much more selective. And then came the large-scale November downturn that further impacted sentiment, but talk of an “AI bubble” is now so far gone that it’s now only frightening small insects. Profits are still very good, data center spending continues unabated, and the use of AI in business processes has become structural.

This does not mean that there are not some areas where excesses exist. Some of the segments within AI are clearly overvalued. That being said, it is a big leap to say that the entire AI ecosystem is in a bubble. The S&P 500 is at nominal high levels, and I believe that the risk premium today is much more associated with geopolitical events than with a reevaluation of AI as a long term trend. Therefore, if we assume that investing will continue, then it is reasonable to ponder whether a company delivering vital infrastructure can actually stop growing, or if the current price weakness of the stock is primarily reflective of a story/multiple adjustment—rather than a real decline in fundamentals.

What I want from the next quarterly earnings report

With the upcoming quarterly earnings, I'm not asking for much from the company. I believe Arista Networks will record at least a slight increase in both revenue and margins compared to previous quarters. The past guidance is already priced in, so even slightly improving estimates would be very helpful in this market phase. While I continue to believe the data center investment cycle is quite solid, I've realized that over the past year it has become much more fragmented and non-linear than in the past. Therefore, the only thing I ask from the company is clean execution—nothing excessive, but no more mistakes. If you make a mistake, the market punishes you, and it hurts. Take Intel (INTC), for example, which, after its (more or less good) quarterly results fell 15%. At other times, the stock would have risen. Now the market demands perfect execution.

Therefore, prudent yet consistent guidance by management should go a long way to removing much of the uncertainty that exists in regards to the stock price today.

While there are many issues that could impact Arista Networks' upcoming quarterly results, margins remain the most sensitive of all, due to the fact that Arista is highly esteemed as a quality leader, and it must continue to live up to this reputation. As such, when the market places Arista on the pedestal as a quality leader, even a relatively small squeeze is viewed as an extreme negative event. For this reason, while I am not looking for a complete recovery of margins immediately, I am interested in understanding whether the margin pressures estimated for Q4 were simply the result of a one-time mix adjustment phase or something that may be more structural in nature. If the answer is the former, then the risk/reward ratio becomes very attractive, very quickly.

Valuation

Arista is not going to show a decline in its earnings per share (EPS) trajectory. In fact, I think we are seeing normalization of EPS; that is a completely different thing. A lot of stocks have gotten carried away recently, thanks to the AI hype, and the market is simply requiring them to monetize those earnings.

The EPS went from $2.203 in fiscal year 2024 to $2.86 in fiscal year 2025. Fiscal year 2025 was the first time we saw true operating leverage and the impact of AI. After that, According to analysts' estimates (see next chart), EPS should continue to rise, in a rational and predictable manner, to $3.34 in fiscal year 2026, $3.96 in fiscal year 2027, and $4.68 in fiscal year 2028.

As I mentioned previously and in prior articles, the time of rapid growth rewarded by the market is behind us. Today Wall Street wants evidence, not just stories. If a company is considered "expensive," then the company needs to be "perfect." Mistakes are no longer acceptable. Therefore, when the P/E ratio is no longer sufficient to justify an elevated price for a stock, then the company itself must justify that price. How? For me, there must be solid financial performance. Simple. That is why I focus on and will continue to focus on EPS. As long as the EPS trend upward quarter-by-quarter, the earnings will perform the function of absorbing the price, reducing the stretch on the stock, and providing an attractive risk/return without the need for another "AI excitement". Of course, there may be some missteps along the way, that is natural.

When it comes to the target price, I continue to give Arista a premium because in this space, Arista is one of the only companies to combine size, visibility, and discipline. For the target, I'm using 2027 EPS at $3.99, which is far enough ahead to incorporate cycle normalization, but not so far away as to become impossible. With a 47x P/E multiple applied to the forecasted 2027 EPS of $3.99, based on the quality profile of the company and the current valuation, I arrive at a target of approximately $187 (+35% from current levels).

The target does not assume any additional multiple expansion. All Arista has to do is provide the market with what it expects of leaders today: execute well. It is obvious that the road to achieving the target price could be difficult, particularly since multiples are high across the board (not just for ANET, but for the entire market). Therefore, it is possible, and I am accounting for this, that the stock will encounter some pauses or corrections along the way. Nevertheless, the buying opportunity that exists today is quite compelling (see the next chart).

Main Risks

NVIDIA (NVDA) poses a serious risk because if customers start buying everything together (chips, network, and software) from a single supplier, would be that Arista may lose participation in a number of deals. Although there are many factors that could influence this (for example, the fact that this comes at a significant cost), this risk alone has the potential to affect a handful of large projects, and therefore could have an impact on the stock.

A further consideration is that Arista is selected today because of its reliability and ability to get into production quickly. Should the capex cycle slow down, and costs become a larger factor than speed of delivery, as I do hope for a general return to normalcy in costs, some of these customers may look for less expensive options (white box), and this could cause the valuation to drop. It will ultimately depend on the ability of the management team to develop a solution to the problem.

Lastly, I think they need to find ways to deal with customer concentration. Arista is dependent upon sales to several large cloud providers. If one of these providers were to take a pause, or delay orders by a couple of months, the market would interpret this as a sign of reduced demand, and therefore the stock could decline even though the fundamentals of the company had not changed.

Conclusion

Arista does not need to win at AI nor be the best in its class; as long as it continues to do well in an already entrenched position within the ecosystem, the stock has been trending upwards recently, as I said before. However, the investing journey in 2026 appears to be significantly more selective and focused. Q3 was a positive quarter for the company; however, Q4 will truly be the test of the company. While I believe the recent decline (November) was a multiple adjustment rather than a sign of structural problems, we need a confirmation. Therefore, I will reassess based on the results of the company's quarterly reports. Regardless, I like the stock. If the company provides clear guidance for the future and the margin pressures start to stabilize (without being 'wow'), I believe there is a strong likelihood of a price re-rating. The 187-190 range should be defensible based solely on the fact that all that is required for this to occur is for the estimates for 2026-27 to remain intact and the company to not make any poor decisions that may tarnish the history of this company.

data.title: Carvana: The Age Of Profitability
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments & Shivansh Prashant Mundra, Investment Research Intern @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis of Carvana (CVNA) in July 2021, we believed its aggressive expansion strategy of reconditioning centers would support rapid sales growth. We also highlighted that Carvana was gaining economies of scale, with gross profit per unit rising from 1.7% in 2016 to 7.6% in 2020, and expected margins to keep on rising. It has had a rough patch since then, with its stock declining by 99% in the span of 18 months and staging a major recovery and surge of over 11,400% since its bottom back in the end of 2022.

We examine the Carvana to explain this impressive turnaround in terms of its revenue performance and financial profile, which went through an uncertain period but has been improving recently. We review its growth outlook, which remains positive amid unit sales recovery and a favorable environment as auto loan rates decline, and more importantly, efforts to improve its financials and profitability, which could support further upside.

Revenue Growth Recovering

We first analyze Carvana’s revenue growth performance over the past years and break it down in terms of unit volume sales and average selling prices ('ASP'). We also compare it against the US market growth trend to assess its relative growth performance.

Carvana's revenue growth trend above has seen strong fluctuations over the period analyzed, with strong and impressive growth that was mostly above 100% except in 2020 due to robust unit sales volume growth mainly. In 2022, its growth performance tumbled and slowed to an average as unit sales growth was flattish. This is as the US retail sales weakened with only a growth rate of 0.02% as the economy was dealing with high inflation and the automotive market experienced unprecedented supply chain issues. At the same time, higher rates made it difficult for consumers to stomach the higher financing costs, which hurts automotive players like Carvana. The company also entered 2022 with “higher than normalized inventory size relative to sales volumes”, as stated by the management, as stated by the management in its previous Q4 2022 earnings call.

But in 2024 and last year, Carvana's revenue growth performance rebounded (45.5% YoY) in Q3 2025 TTM, as its sales volume growth picked up. There are also favorable macro tailwinds as interest rates are falling, leading to lower finance rates on auto loans, which is positive for Carvana.

The P/S ratio of Carvana has been very volatile, with the ratio growing to a multiple of more than 3.6x at its peak in 2021. The spike could be attributed to market enthusiasm for tech-enabled growth stocks during the pandemic, and the company’s online platform to buy and sell cars became highly attractive. The ratio dropped significantly to almost 0 between 2021-2023 as revenue and sales slowed down in 2022 and 2023. Since the stock price and Carvana’s sales have witnessed a rebound, fueled by the restructuring taken up by the management. The ratio currently sits at a multiple of 3.53x.

We expect the revenue for Carvana to grow at an average of 26% over the next five years with a declining growth rate, which is more or less in line with analyst expectations for the company, supported by continued recovery in unit sales volume growth, and also falling auto finance rates may entice consumers to purchase vehicles. We expect the revenue to grow also because of their recent investments into AI-driven platforms that will help them optimize efficiency. The bulk of the growth would be fueled by growth in the number of units sold by CVNA. To forecast the growth rate of units sold, we took the average of the increase in units sold in 2024 and 2025. To forecast the increase in units being sold in 2025, we prorated it based on its H1 performance. To forecast the growth in the ASP, we took the average growth of ASP over the last eight years but excluded the post pandemic years (2021 and 2022) as we believe the high-growth rate in those two years was an exception.

Improving Margins and Profitability

In the next section, we analyze Carvana’s margins and profitability. We look at the companies’ margins chart and expense table to understand the trend.

Based on the table above, gross margins have been steadily improving from 1.38% in 2015 to 21.89% in TTM. EBIT margins and net margins have also followed the same growth trend, with EBIT and net margins breaking even in 2024 and 2023, respectively. FCF margins have also experienced improving growth and finally turned positive in 2023. Overall, the company’s margins have been showing consistent improvement over the last 10 years, which we expect to continue moving forward.

The company’s margins have turned profitable after years of unprofitability amid its strong expansion drive. Based on the expense analysis chart, the improvement is mainly driven by lower COGS and SG&A as a percentage of total revenue. COGS declined from 96.5% in 2015 to 80.7% in 2024, while SG&A declined from 28.5% to 14.9% over the same period. This is as management shifted its focus on sustainable profitability rather than chasing high growth and expansion through its three-step restructuring plan. It also used AI like its "Carli" AI-driven platform for vehicle reconditioning, to maximize efficiency. In its previous earnings call, management stated it managed to reduce operational costs per unit ($1,549 per vehicle) by 8.7% in Q2 of 2025 YoY due to these efficiencies, and management also highlighted that SG&A per retail unit sold continued to fall in Q3 2025 in its latest earnings transcript, which shows it continues to focus on profitability improvement.

Carvana’s stock price initially surged from 2018 to a high of $376 in 2021, before declining significantly in 2022. The sudden decline in stock prices was also followed by worsening net income. In 2023, the company finally turned profitable, aligning with improving stock prices. In 2024 and in 2025, both stock prices and earnings have been robustly trending upwards.

The company has made visible efforts to improve its efficiency and optimize usage of AI-driven platforms to improve its margins and turn the company profitable. We expect CVNA to continue on this path of improving its margins and driving growth, fueled by the restructuring efforts of the management. Financials show its SG&A expenses has declined from the peak in 2022 ($2.49 billion) to $1.71 billion in 2024, which is a major improvement as a % of revenue by 5.8%. The operational efficiencies have also been felt by the company in Q2 of 2025, with total SG&A Expense per Unit on a GAAP basis decreased to $3,846 in Q2 2025, down $639 (14.2%) from $4,485 in Q2 2024 based on its filing. We model Carvana’s net margins to continue improving going forward, supported by efficiency gains and scale benefits. Specifically, we project a 5-year forward average net margin of 12%, driven mainly by sustained improvements in both COGS and SG&A as the company continues to optimize operations and expand its unit volumes.

Improved Financial Position With Reduced Debt and Higher Cash

In the last section, we analyze the improved financial position of the company. The company has managed to improve its cash-to-debt ratio, and we analyze the reasons behind it and whether it is sustainable. We also look at Carvana’s price-to-book ratio as well to analyze the trend better.

Carvana had a very volatile cash-to-debt ratio between 2015-2018 as the debt levels and cash and cash equivalents level were not very high. The ratio stabilized more from 2018 holding mainly at a level of 0.1x. One of the reasons for their high debt levels in 2022 is the acquisition of ADESA, a used car auction company. To finance the acquisition, the company took on debt of $3.3 billion. The ratio has improved significantly since 2023, holding a level of 0.4x. This was caused mainly due to an increase in cash levels at the company. The debt levels dropped in 2023 as the company executed a landmark $5.5 billion debt exchange in mid-2023, reducing its outstanding debt by 20%, capturing discounts from bonds that were trading below 40 cents on the dollar in certain tranches. This pushed out maturities, including, most importantly, a US$500m 2025 bond maturity and reduced cash interest by $910 million over two years. With more cost-cutting and efficiency efforts, CVNA was able to improve cash flow from core business activities and enable higher cash generation in 2024. Carvana has also switched interest payments on much of its restructured debt to "paid-in-kind", which has temporarily suspended cash outflows. This reduced interest payments of $274 million in 2024, as stated in its annual report.

CVNA has managed to improve its financial health over the last two years by restructuring the company's debt. Entering a major debt exchange worth $5.5 billion helped them reduce their total outstanding debt by 20%. Carvana was also able to push out the maturity of bonds that were due soon, helping them lower short-term refinancing pressures. These measures have had a huge impact on its cash-to-debt ratio, improving it a great deal. The increase in cash flow from higher margins and their Paid-in-Kind interest has also affected the ratio. We believe these measures have positioned the company well to pay back its debt. Based on our forecasts, we modelled its 5-year FCF margin to be 12.08% (capex only), which will help improve its cash balances and improve its ratio even more.

Risk: Competition

One of the risks that could be a threat to Carvana is the highly competitive used car reselling market, which is very fragmented, with the top 20 used car retailers having less than 20% market share in the US according to McKinsey. Used cars are typical products and not specialized;thus product differentiation is limited. The barrier to entry of the used car market is also low with minimal capital requirements compared to a automaker with large factories, making it relatively easy for new players to enter the market.

Valuation

To value Carvana, we use a P/E ratio valuation due to its strong profit growth while revenue growth is expected to moderate. Based on its current P/E ratio, which we applied to the 2025 period, prorating it down gradually to the 5-year average top US Automotive Retail industry ratio of 15.27x by 2030, our model shows an upside of 41.6% for 2026 with a price target of $663.12.

Verdict

Carvana’s strong turnaround is supported by a recovery of its revenue growth and improving financial profile. After facing a steep contraction in growth compared to past years pre-2022, it has shown a positive revenue growth rebound as unit sales accelerated. More importantly in our view, we believe its stock remains attractive due to management's shift to focusing on profitability through various initiatives, which could lead to further gains in its margins. Based on our P/E valuation, our model shows continued upside as Carvana continues to focus on profitability improvement and grow in scale; thus, we rate the company as a Strong Buy with a price target of $663.12.

data.title: DFIV: Buying International Value Without Falling Into Value Traps
data.description:
The Dimensional International Value ETF (DFIV) is an active ex-US developed value strategy that generally delivers consistent alpha over other passive international value ETFs. This outperformance is driven by its active methodology and portfolio selection that shows indications of high-quality value picks without being caught in value traps.

I consider DFIV a decent pick within its value international segment. However, investors should be aware of what they are signing up for. International diversification is often not about chasing growth, and DFIV investors often need multi-year holding periods to translate value into returns. The expected uncorrelated positioning with respect to US portfolios is not seen in times of global crises, but these ETFs do stabilize the portfolio through lower drawdowns.

DFIV is a buy only for investors who are clear about what international developed economies value exposure is about. Within the group, DFIV is expected to maximize risk-adjusted returns.

Methodology Implications

DFIV is a systematic, research-led value strategy implemented actively. The portfolio managers select large cap developed ex-US companies that trade at low relative prices, primarily defined by price-to-book, with secondary valuation anchors such as price-to-earnings and price-to-cash-flow. However, the focus is not just on value. Higher weight tilts are assigned to companies with higher profitability (earnings or operating profits relative to book value or assets). This is where DFIV balances cheap with economically resilient businesses.

The portfolio is also not blinded by a backward-looking metric like market cap (though that too can work and has for US growth in the past). This introduces potential for alpha generation by including active bets on smaller companies within the large cap universe. The active management also keeps short-term adjustments in view and continuously reacts to price momentum changes and reversals while balancing turnover.

For taxable investors, DFIV's tax emphasis, with a focus on delaying realized capital gains, tax loss harvesting, and biasing long-term tax treatments, should work out better in terms of post-tax returns - particularly compared to passive and mechanically operated ETFs.

The factsheet I downloaded from the DFIV website shows how the portfolio balances cheaper valuations with profitability. DFIV's price-to-book ratio is ~1.34x, far lower than the benchmark (MSCI World ex USA Value Index (net div.)). Not signaling distressed valuations, DFIV's valuations still indicate significant valuation discounts compared to the benchmark at levels that should matter. This gap in valuations does not mean the gap in profitability is commensurate. DFIV's weighted average profitability metric is only modestly behind the benchmark's. Overall, DFIV shops for value names, but not at the cost of economic quality - avoiding value traps effectively. This is primarily achieved through a larger allocation towards smaller caps (within the large cap universe) - something visible from the weighted average market cap difference.

Portfolio Walkthrough

I downloaded the full holdings from the DFIV website and did not see any explicit currency hedge sleeve. DFIV therefore remains susceptible to strong USD regimes versus local currencies.

The portfolio is heavily diversified - not a high conviction portfolio, although active. The top 10 holdings account for only ~18% of the portfolio. For a comparable portfolio size (~500 stocks), the S&P 500 has ~38% in the top 10. This means, DFIV is not as dependent on stock selection as on systematic broad market behavior.

The portfolio is, at first glance, a collection of large and boring stocks, but that is the intent. DFIV is not designed for growth and relies heavily on cash generating stocks. This is reflected in the sectoral allocation too, where banks, old economy consumer staples, and capital intensive companies dominate. Despite being expectedly overweight on financials, it still allocates materially lower weights here compared to the international value benchmark, indicating selectivity. DFIV allocates relatively more in energy and materials - usually cheaper in global indices.

The higher allocation in consumer discretionary is tilted toward value plays - mature discretionary bets in autos and legacy brands (not growth discretionary). This adds cyclical upside outside financials and commodities, diversifying cyclical risks. The country allocation does not alter the benchmark allocations too much, anchoring base return expectations around the benchmark while attempting to add active alpha - not a materially different return profile altogether, as seen through all allocation lenses from geographical split to sectoral.

Performance and Use Cases

The most important chart for DFIV is its total return comparison with EFV. That chart shows exactly the kind of outperformance in DFIV that tilts the thesis to a Buy on its own. The outperformance is across regimes and consistent, without meaningfully amplifying drawdowns (like in 2020). This adds confidence to the active management's ability to consistently deliver alpha using its value-profitability balance without deviating from the thematic structure in terms of allocation.

The second chart of importance is the rolling 3 year total return chart. While the CAGR return (including dividends reinvested) is an impressive 12%, most of the upside happens in spurts. Much of the total return gains in the last 10 years have only come since 2024. And there are several periods of broadly flat performance. Investors who entered at the 2018 peaks did not see positive returns till 2024. This is reflected in the 3 year rolling return charts too, where DFIV goes through significant periods of flat to negative 3-year returns. That should set expectations on holding tenures investors need to sign up for. Investors who hold over a full cycle are rewarded handsomely, but value markets can remain subdued for long periods of time.

A general expectation from value portfolios is relative stability. Here too, expectations should be moderated. DFIV fell almost 19% during the peak-to-trough decline seen in 2022. In the same period, SPY was down by ~24%. So DFIV does not necessarily act like a defensive across all correction periods. That is true, especially if the drawdown is due to macro factors like rates (as was the case in 2022). The drawdown mitigation works out better in corrections that pressure valuations (like during the COVID crash) - not in 2022 like drawdowns where higher rates adversely impact DFIV's cyclical exposure too.

The correlation with SPY also shows healthy and supportive deviations, particularly when valuation led rallies peak, or during valuation led corrections. This keeps the hedging/portfolio stabilizing idea intact- both because of DFIV's geographical and style diversification.

DFIV's yield on cost chart reflects the cyclicality of international value sectors as well as the recent surge in share prices (since 2024). A stronger USD also dampens payouts. While yields are still healthy at around 3% levels today, it is a by-product of the value investing objective. Income levels could fluctuate across cycles.

Outlook and Recommendation

Within the international value space, DFIV definitely works out well and is likely to continue outperforming passive market cap biased methodologies. This is possible because DFIV balances smaller large cap exposure to achieve a visible value-profitability balance. The ETF also shows reasonable diversification benefits in broad market crashes, unless the corrections are driven by macro factors that impact cyclicals as much.

Going ahead, with US markets starting to slow down on growth themes, and with valuation pressures undeniably high, the role for DFIV in core portfolios becomes important. Not because the next few years are going to be value outperforming growth, but because it stabilizes the portfolio - more required in high-valuation regimes. Heightened geopolitical uncertainties today also support the case for international diversification.

DFIV is a decent Buy for those who want to stabilize valuation risks, not volatility. DFIV can go through its own volatile phases and may suffer from global macro risk exposure, not US concentration risks. DFIV will not protect portfolios against global recessions driven by cyclicals. It will not outperform in narrow, US-led growth rallies and will not necessarily eliminate drawdowns. Overall, DFIV should be viewed as a strategic allocation for long-term investors on grounds of return quality, diversification, and valuation balance - not as a near term bet on value leadership or as a substitute for defensive vehicles.

Risks to the Thesis

Having discussed the positives, DFIV is exposed to risks that could delay or moderate expected outcomes (some of which we have already discussed, but collating here). Since DFIV is exposed to the international value factor, it could go through years of consolidation and underperformance, similar to what we saw in 2017-23.

DFIV can underperform as sharply as broader markets in regimes of higher interest rates, tighter financial conditions, or global growth slowdowns that pressure cyclicals. Sustained periods of USD strength against developed market currencies can weigh on both total returns and income. Many developed economies face structural headwinds from an aging demographic and lower growth that could limit rerating. They tend to prioritize balance sheet stability over aggressive capital returns too.

Recent outperformance leads to crowding risks, as quality factors may have become widely sought after to hedge against growth vulnerabilities after a sustained period of growth surge. The active management style is also prone to underperformance in unfavorable market conditions (although not observed so far). The value-profitability balance should also be kept in mind - DFIV is not likely to rally as strongly (if pure value tilts are what you are looking for) in regimes where pure value is surging.

data.title: 2026 Year-Ahead Outlook: Macro, Equities, Funds, RMBS, CMBS And CLO Insights
data.description:
By Tajinder Dhillon, CFA, Senior Manager, Earnings and Equity Research | Luke Lu, Head of Credit Research and Quantitative Modelling | Plamen Mitkov, Global RMBS Research Team Lead | Detlef Glow, Head of EMEA Research at LSEG Lipper | Robin Marshall, Director, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell | Irene Shi, RMBS Modeller | Dewi John, Lipper Research Lead, Europe | Jharonne Martis, Director, Consumer Research

Macro

Although there remain global risks, including geopolitical, outright recession risks in the US and globally appear relatively low in 2026, and compelling evidence of bubbles in credit and equities is hard to find in conventional valuation measures. Global financial conditions have eased steadily since the tariff spike in April 2025, and central banks have signalled they do not wish to shrink balance sheets to pre-GFC levels to reduce financial stability risks. Inflation is still above some central bank targets, but there is little evidence of a return to a high inflation regime. Thus, the US Fed has scope to ease policy further if required, should unemployment increase faster in 2026. In Europe, with ECB rates already at 2%, more active use of German fiscal policy is an important supplement to demand growth. Longer-dated government bond yields near post-GFC highs, and above 5% in some maturities, also discount substantial debt/GDP ratios and funding burdens in the G7.

Figure 1: selected long-dated G7 govt bond yields

Equities

Looking ahead to 2026, fundamentals remain supportive, driven by a) resilient earnings, which are expected to broaden outside of the Magnificent 7, b) profit margins near all-time highs, c) potential operating leverage improvement as AI adoption drives productivity and cost efficiencies, and d) Fed Funds futures are pricing in two to three rate cuts this year.

A key risk outside of the macro risks highlighted above lies in investor patience with AI’s return on investment and whether hyperscalers are over-investing for uncertain payoffs. With valuations near levels last seen during the internet bubble, volatility around earnings and AI enablers could dictate equity price trajectories in the near term.

Figure 2: Capital Expenditures for Major Hyperscalers

Retail consumer

The LSEG Retail/Restaurant Index projects moderate growth for 2025, following two years of double-digit gains. Earnings are expected to rise 5.9% and revenue 4.6% for the year, supported by resilient consumer demand. Q3 2025 delivered 8.2% earnings growth, while Q4 is forecasted to slow to 1.1%. Looking ahead, 2026 growth momentum is set to accelerate, with earnings projected to climb 10.9% and revenue 5.8%, signaling a positive trajectory for the sector.

Figure 3: The LSEG Retail/Restaurant Earnings Index: Q1 2024 Act – Q4 2026 Est.

Funds

Money Market USD funds dominated flows in 2025. Their appeal persisted despite reinverted yield curves late in 2024, driven by the Federal Reserve’s high interest rates, which kept MMF yields above 4–5%, outperforming bank deposits. Non-US investors also favoured MMFs due to the US’ higher rates, despite dollar weakness in early 2025.

ETFs dominated equity flows (+$502bn), overshadowing mutual funds. Equity US funds fell from second place in 2024 to eighth in 2025, with inflows about 20% of the previous year, along with a huge rotation from mutual funds into ETFs in this classification. US flows were volatile, swinging from heavy outflows in May to strong inflows in November. European enthusiasm faded after late-2024 spikes, with large redemptions over the summer, while Asian investors remained consistently positive.

Equity Global ranked second in Europe and Asia but stayed negative in the US, while Equity Europe rebounded in 2025 after prior sell-offs, while U.S. investors diversified into Europe and Canada. Equity US Small & Mid Cap suffered the worst redemptions, hurt by concerns over tariffs, high rates, and weak growth.

Figure 4: Global largest inflows and redemptions to mutual funds and ETFs globally, January to November 2025 (USD bn)

Agency RMBS

The Agency RMBS market is expected to remain generally resilient in 2026, supported by steady issuance, tempered housing price growth, and potential declines in mortgage rates. Recent policy initiatives, such as agency MBS purchases, restrictions on institutional buyers, GSE reforms, VantageScore 4.0 adoption, and mortgage portability, could influence affordability and liquidity, but their effectiveness and timing remain uncertain. Prepayment activity is expected to stay muted for deeply out-of-the-money cohorts, while in-the-money and modestly OTM cohorts may see some pickup if mortgage rates decline further.

Overall, the combination of stable issuance, supportive policy measures, and incremental improvements in affordability points to a cautiously optimistic outlook for the Agency RMBS sector in 2026.

Non-Agency RMBS

2025 has been a very strong year for the Non-Agency public and private market, with an increase in issuance of 42% compared to 2024, led by the uptake in the Non-QM sector. In a stable macroeconomic environment in 2026, further Non-Agency issuance growth may continue.

As the mortgage rates started to ease in the second half of 2025, we have seen an increase in prepayments for the 2023 – 2025 origination vintages for Jumbo and Non-QM. This cohort has originated in considerably higher rates and has greater incentives to refinance at the new interest rates. We expect an increase in prepayments until mid-2026 especially for the Prime Jumbo mortgages.

Credit risk has been stable in 2025, and with stabilization of inflation and a strong job market, the outlook for the credit risk on the Non-Agency mortgages is stable for 2026

Figure 5: Non-Agency Issuance

CMBS - Agency CMBS

Agency CMBS had a strong year in 2025, with issuance rising more than 34% YoY amid rate cuts and benign financing conditions. Spreads were largely stable for most products, except a short-lived widening in April tied to tariff distress. However, multifamily property fundamentals were softer in vacancy and rent. Meanwhile, credit performance was generally resilient, and prepayment speeds remained muted in general given limited refinance incentives.

The base case scenario assumes gradual rate normalization, stable capital market demand, and slow improvement in fundamentals as supply recedes—implying stable issuance, range‑bound spreads, and steady credit and prepayment performance.

CMBS - Non-Agency CMBS

The CMBS market achieved post-GFC record issuance in 2025 on improved liquidity and sentiment in CRE, despite mixed macroeconomic conditions. CRE fundamentals continued the recovery, though the momentum has slowed down in rent and cash flow growth and the recovery remained highly uneven. Meanwhile, property valuations have finally stabilized after a three-year slide, even though some credit metrics continued to deteriorate.

To frame the outlook for 2026, we anchor major CMBS themes to the expected rate path and broader economic conditions, with two key scenarios on focus:

  1. A baseline scenario where the Federal Reserve delivers 1-2 additional rate cuts for the year and inflation continues to ease gradually with the labour market softening but remaining resilient
  2. A stress scenario of stagflation where labour market deterioration accelerates and unemployment climbs above 5% while inflation remains sticky or even rekindles, putting Fed in a conundrum.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLO)

As we move into 2026, we see major headwinds, including elevated inflation and input costs and a weakening labour market, which may slow economic growth and pressure leveraged loan credit fundamentals. While we expect continued rate cuts to support investor sentiment, lower policy rates may reduce demand for floating rate products. On the bright side, a resurgence in M&A and LBO activities will boost leveraged loan supply, supporting issuance and arbitrage. Additionally, deregulation in financial market may fuel bank demand.

Overall, we have a constructive outlook for the CLO market in 2026. Further Fed easing, sustained investor demand, and stabilizing credit fundamentals provide a solid foundation for continued market growth. Meanwhile, default and downgrade activities are expected to continue, albeit at a more moderate pace compared to recent years. Credit dispersion will remain a key theme with risks concentrated in selected industries facing tariff-related pressure or AI disruption, particularly among issuers with weaker balance sheets and elevated leverage. As such, asset analysis and manager selection remain critical for CLO investment with a focus on tail risk mitigation.
